items.misconception: -1, by computing 5 - 2 x 3 and ignoring the negative.
items.why: Students drop the negative sign during substitution or fail to use brackets, leading to sign errors.

items.misconception: 8xy, by combining all terms regardless of variable.
items.random_wrong: 9xy, by adding all the numbers and merging the letters.
items.why: Students treat different variables as interchangeable, missing that only terms with the same variable (and power) can be combined.

items.q: Can Pythagoras' theorem be used on any triangle?
items.correct: No — it only applies to right-angled triangles.
items.misconception: Yes — it works on any triangle as long as you square the sides.
items.random_wrong: Only on equilateral triangles because all sides are equal.
items.why: Students learn the formula a^2 + b^2 = c^2 abstractly and forget the essential prerequisite — a 90 degree angle.

items.q: A particle starts from rest and accelerates at 4 m/s² for 3 seconds. Final velocity is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 12 m/s. “7 m/s” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which SUVAT equation does NOT involve t?
items.why: Correct answer: v² = u² + 2as. “v = u + at” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If u = 0 m/s, a = 10 m/s², find s after 5 s.
items.why: The real figure is 125 m. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
